Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Still 5 green cells, no G/g overlays. The merge strategy I tried before doesn't seem to work now. Maybe the board was reset to the initial state and the merge mechanic is different than I thought.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. Looking at the current state:
- 5 green cells at (19,18), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42)
- No overlays or special indicators
Maybe the game requires a different sequence of actions. Let me try:
1. Click on a green cell to select it
2. Press RIGHT arrow to move it to the right
3. Repeat until all green cells are in the same column
Let me try selecting (19,36) and pressing RIGHT multiple times to move it to column 42.
Actually, I already tried pressing RIGHT after selecting a green cell and it didn't move. Let me try a different approach.
Maybe I need to click on the gray cells to move the green cells. Let me try clicking on the gray cell at (19,42) to see if it moves the green cell from (19,36) to (19,42).
Wait, I already tried that and it didn't work.
Let me try clicking on the green cell at (19,36) multiple times to see if it does something.
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to click on all green cells in a specific order. Let me try clicking on them from left to right: (19,18), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on all green cells in order
result = action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 18},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 24},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 36},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 25, 'col': 42},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 37, 'col': 42}
])
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
green_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green: {len(green_nodes)}")
for n in green_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    cr = sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b)
    cc = sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b)
    print(f"  center=({cr},{cc})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
